title: Red Wine on Silk
timeEstimate: Pro-lane within hours; home is damage-prevention only
stainChemistry: Red wine tannins grab onto silk fibers quickly. The alcohol can weaken delicate silk if it warms up. Silk dyes run easily and the fiber is fragile, so everything stays cold and gentle to avoid setting the stain or damaging the material. The shorthand: silk plus red wine is a pro-lane case within hours; home is damage-prevention before the pro sees it.
whyThisWorks: Immediate cold water flushes out most of the wine and lifts the tannins before they lock in. A specialist can then use a controlled mild acid and reducing bleach to clear what's left without damaging the fiber. Home chemistry has too many edge cases on silk — most household products damage the fiber faster than the wine does. Blotting instead of rubbing keeps the stain from driving deeper. No heat ever — heat sets it permanently.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): wine pigment is anthocyanin plus condensed tannins, pH-sensitive. Silk's fibroin protein offers hydrogen-bonding sites where tannins anchor. Heat above ~30°C denatures fibroin. Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) reverses oxidized tannin without the yellowing peroxide causes on silk dyes.
defaultAssumption: Silk + red wine = pro within hours. Home goal is damage prevention, not stain removal. The garment has a much better outcome if it arrives at the cleaner with nothing applied to it.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold/ice water, white cotton towel under
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water through the BACK of the stain over a white catch towel. Press, do not scrub. Most fresh anthocyanin transfers in the first 60–90 seconds when caught immediately.
Apply diluted acetic acid via dropper to the residual stain. Press with white cloth — never rub. 60-second ceiling on silk. Flush with cold water immediately.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Sodium hydrosulfite solution per pro reference
spottingProtocol.instruction: For oxidized brown residue only, after acid step. Reducing agent reverses tannin oxidation safely on silk where peroxide would yellow. Strict 60-sec dwell, then flush thoroughly with cold water and NSD.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold Water Final Rinse + Air-Dry Flat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush from back to front until rinse water is clear. Press between dry white towels. Lay flat to air-dry, out of sun, never hang wet.

steps: 2. Diluted acetic acid spotter, 60-sec dwell, cold rinse.
3. Sodium hydrosulfite for oxidized residue, 60-sec dwell, thorough rinse.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets silk + sets tannin
NEVER enzymes — Protein digests silk protein
NEVER hydrogen peroxide — yellows silk dyes
Never wring or scrub wet silk

homeSolutions.agent: Honest Answer — Silk + Red Wine Is a Pro Job
homeSolutions.instruction: Silk is the fragile fiber where most home stain advice causes more damage than the stain. Red wine on silk is no exception. The household tools that work on cotton — OxiClean, hot water, scrubbing, peroxide — all damage silk. Even vinegar over-dwells past 60 seconds and weakens the fiber. The honest path is: get the spill under control with cold water, leave the rest alone, and take it to a cleaner who specializes in silk WITHIN HOURS. The garment's best outcome is the one where you bring it in with nothing else applied.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Blot from the BACK — That's It
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn the garment inside out. Press a clean white towel under the stain. Run COLD water (ice cold preferred) over the BACK of the stain into the catch towel. Cold only — anything warmer than tap-cold sets the tannin and risks setting the silk. Do NOT scrub, do NOT wring, do NOT pour from above. The water should run THROUGH the silk into the towel. 60 seconds of patient flushing usually pulls fresh wine to a pale shadow — and that's all you should attempt at home.

homeSolutions.agent: Press Dry — DO NOT Apply Anything Else
homeSolutions.instruction: Press the silk between two dry white towels to lift moisture. No wringing. No twisting. No hanging wet. Lay flat to air-dry at room temperature, out of sun. Do NOT apply vinegar, peroxide, OxiClean, alcohol, club soda, or salt. Every one of those has a way of damaging silk that's worse than the wine. If a pink shadow remains, that's what the pro is for.

homeSolutions.agent: Get to a Silk-Specialist Cleaner Within Hours
homeSolutions.instruction: Not every dry cleaner handles silk. CALL FIRST and ask: "Do you treat silk with sodium hydrosulfite for oxidized tannin?" If they don't understand the question, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them: "Red wine on silk, age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No acid, no peroxide, no detergent applied." That handoff is valuable — pros can go straight to acetic acid + reducing bleach with confidence the silk hasn't been compromised.

steps: 1. Silk + red wine = pro-lane within hours.
2. Cold water back-flush from the reverse, into a catch towel.
3. Press dry between white towels — no other chemistry.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after the cold-water back-flush. Anything else home-applied risks worse damage than the wine.
warnings: NEVER vinegar over 60 seconds (and only with hidden-area test)
NEVER scrub or wring wet silk
Pour through, not on top — water rings are as visible as wine

neverDo: Never wring or scrub wet silk
fiberSensitivities: Silk is protein fiber — same enzyme-digestion risk as wool/cashmere
Water rings if applied carelessly — pour through, not on top
Aggressive friction ruptures wet silk fibers

materialWarnings: Pro-lane within hours — home is damage-prevention only
Cold water back-flush is the entire home protocol
No vinegar, no peroxide, no OxiClean at home

escalation.when: Always — silk + red wine is pro-lane within hours of the spill, regardless of stain age or apparent severity.
escalation.whatToTell: "Red wine on silk, age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No acid, no peroxide, no detergent applied."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in silk (call ahead — confirm sodium hydrosulfite + acetic acid spotting capability)

contextQuestions: How long ago — minutes, hours, or older?
Type of silk — charmeuse, chiffon, habotai, dupioni?
Has anyone applied anything yet (water / vinegar / club soda / salt)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Thinner silks (chiffon, habotai) are more vulnerable than charmeuse or dupioni. Anything pre-applied at home (especially salt or club soda) makes the pro's job harder — disclose honestly. Older oxidized stains drift toward brown and need reducing bleach; that's pro-only chemistry on silk.
